content.full_text:
WARD, JONES, AGUIRRE & SEIDENWURM
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING PROFESSIONAL CORPORATIONS
ATTORNEYS AT LAW
2100 FINANCIAL SQUARE
400 B STREET
SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA 92101

A. MORGAN JONES
WILLIAM O. WARD, III*
RICHARD L. SEIDENWURM*
GARY J. AGUIRRE*
WILLIAM W. RAVIN
PAUL P. SMITH

TELEPHONE 714-233-7803

*A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

May 5, 1976

Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95816

Attention: Regina Kutchins

Gentlemen:

Pursuant to a telephone discussion with Regina Kutchins
and on behalf of a corporate client, we hereby request an informal
opinion of the Commission as to whether our client (the "Corporation")
has made a "contribution" or is a "committee" within the meaning
of the Fair Political Practices Act. The facts, as represented by
the Corporation, are as follows:

1. The Corporation owns a commercial property including
land and a building (the "property"). The property was leased
under a 15-year lease commencing in 1969 at the rental rate of
$2,500 per month. The original tenant vacated the property in
July 1974 and litigation is now pending with respect to the lease.
The property has been vacant since July 1974, except for a short-
term lease terminated by the tenant after six months' occupancy in
August 1975.

2. In March 1976 the Corporation was approached by the
campaign manager of a political candidate (the "candidate"), who
asked if the property was available for rent as a campaign head-
quarters for the candidate. The Corporation indicated that the
property was available, provided that an agreement could be worked
out whereby the Corporation would have the opportunity of terminat-
ing the candidate's tenancy in the event that a more attractive
tenant came along. The campaign manager offered $200 per month as
a rental.

3. The parties entered into a lease pursuant to which:

(a) Occupancy commenced on April 1, 1976;
(b) The term of the lease was 7-1/2 months;
(c) The total rental of $1,500 was paid in advance;
and

(d) The Corporation was given the right to terminate
the lease for any reason on five days' notice and agreed in such

WARD, JONES, AGUIRRE & SEIDENWURM
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING PROFESSIONAL CORPORATIONS
ATTORNEYS AT LAW
2100 FINANCIAL SQUARE
600 B STREET
SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA 92101

A. MORGAN JONES
WILLIAM O. WARD, III*
RICHARD L. SEIDENWURM*
GARY J. AGUIRRE*
WILLIAM W. RAVIN
PAUL B. SMITH

TELEPHONE 714-233-7803

*A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

May 5, 1976

Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95816

Attention: Regina Kutchins

Gentlemen:

Pursuant to a telephone discussion with Regina Kutchins
and on behalf of a corporate client, we hereby request an informal
opinion of the Commission as to whether our client (the "Corporation")
has made a "contribution" or is a "committee" within the meaning
of the Fair Political Practices Act. The facts, as represented by
the Corporation, are as follows:

1. The Corporation owns a commercial property including
land and a building (the "property"). The property was leased
under a 15-year lease commencing in 1969 at the rental rate of
$2,500 per month. The original tenant vacated the property in
July 1974 and litigation is now pending with respect to the lease.
The property has been vacant since July 1974, except for a short-
term lease terminated by the tenant after six months' occupancy in
August 1975.

2. In March 1976 the Corporation was approached by the
campaign manager of a political candidate (the "candidate"), who
asked if the property was available for rent as campaign head-
quarters for the candidate. The Corporation indicated that the
property was available, provided that an agreement could be worked
out whereby the Corporation would have the opportunity of terminat-
ing the candidate's tenancy in the event that a more attractive
tenant came along. The campaign manager offered $200 per month as
a rental.

3. The parties entered into a lease pursuant to which:

(a) Occupancy commenced on April 1, 1976;
(b) The term of the lease was 7-1/2 months;
(c) The total rental of $1,500 was paid in advance;
and

(d) The Corporation was given the right to terminate
the lease for any reason on five days' notice and agreed in such

Fair Political Practices Commission
May 5, 1976
Page Two

case to refund a pro rata share of the entire rental.

4. At the time the lease was negotiated and made, the
officer of the Corporation who negotiated the lease knew nothing
about the political affiliation or views of the candidate or what
office he was running for. The only other officer of the Corporation who participated in the decision to lease the property was
similarly disinterested in the candidate, except as a prospective
source of rental income. Since that time, neither of such officers
nor the Corporation has done anything to support the candidate or
his campaign, and has no intention of doing so.

5. The Corporation's sole purpose in entering into the
transaction was to derive some rental income from the property,
while at the same time maintaining the flexibility to rent the
property on more advantageous terms.

My review of the Fair Political Practices Act leads me to
conclude that the Corporation would have a duty to disclose under
the Act only if it were determined that its entering into the lease
constituted a "contribution" by a "committee". The opinion of the
Commission with regard to these and any other relevant questions,
based on the foregoing facts, is requested. My understanding from
Regina Kutchins is that we can expect an oral opinion from the
Commission within a few days after the Commission's receipt of this
letter. Please feel free to call me at any time.

Very truly yours,

Richard L. Seidenwurm

File No. 12067.01

ROUTE SLIP
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
STD. FORM 118 (REV. 7-72)

DATE
5/11/76

TO:
Ken F                                    760073

ROOM

FROM:
Gina

PHONE

FOR ACTION AS INDICATED
☐ REPLY—MY SIGNATURE          ☐ SIGNATURE           ☐ NOTE AND FORWARD
☐ REPLY—COPY TO ME            ☐ APPROVAL            ☐ NOTE AND FILE
☐ PLEASE SUMMARIZE            ☐ ACTION              ☒ NOTE AND RETURN
☐ PLEASE INVESTIGATE          ☐ COMMENTS            ☐ PLEASE PHONE ME
☐ FORWARDED PER REQUEST       ☐ INFORMATION         ☐ PLEASE SEE ME

REMARKS:
See, I didn't make it up! The added
wrinkle is the opposing candidate's
higher offer for the space. He is
putting it up for bid available to all
candidates for this office. The highest
bid gets the space with the 5 day termin
action clause. Advised him fix he is not
a committee based on these facts.

sections.question: whether our client (the "Corporation") has made a "contribution" or is a "committee" within the meaning of the Fair Political Practices Act
sections.question_synthetic: Does a corporation that leases commercial property to a political candidate's campaign at below-market rates constitute a "contribution" or "committee" under the Fair Political Practices Act?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from attorney Richard L. Seidenwurm on behalf of a corporate client, not an FPPC response. The routing slip indicates FPPC staff advised the client is not a committee based on these facts, but that is marginal notation, not the formal advice letter. Document is from May 5, 1976. No formal conclusion section present in the request letter itself.

embedding.summary: A law firm requests an FPPC informal opinion on behalf of a corporate client regarding whether leasing vacant commercial property to a political candidate's campaign headquarters at $200/month constitutes a reportable contribution or committee formation under the Fair Political Practices Act.
